title: Common Issues - Logical
description: Practice identifying common logical network issues including IP address conflicts, DHCP problems, routing loops, DNS failures, VLAN misconfigurations, duplex mismatches, and logical layer troubleshooting techniques.
starterCode:
# COMMON ISSUES - LOGICAL EXERCISE

## Part 1: IP Address Issues

1. An IP address _______ occurs when two devices on the same network are assigned the same IP address.
2. IP conflicts cause intermittent _______ for one or both affected devices as they compete for the address.
3. A device with a _______ IP address (169.254.x.x) indicates it failed to obtain an address from DHCP.
4. APIPA stands for Automatic Private IP Address and uses the _______ address range.
5. An incorrect _______ mask causes devices to miscalculate which hosts are on the local network vs remote.
6. Assigning an IP address from the wrong _______ prevents the device from communicating on its intended network.
7. Duplicate _______ addresses on routers can cause packets to be forwarded to the wrong network.

## Part 2: DHCP Problems

8. DHCP _______ exhaustion occurs when all available IP addresses in the scope have been assigned.
9. A _______ DHCP server is an unauthorized server that hands out incorrect network configuration.
10. DHCP _______ snooping is a switch security feature that filters untrusted DHCP messages.
11. A DHCP _______ agent forwards DHCP requests between clients and servers across different subnets.
12. If a client receives DHCP settings with an incorrect default _______, it cannot reach remote networks.
13. DHCP _______ time determines how long a client keeps its assigned IP address before renewal.
14. Short DHCP lease times can cause frequent _______ renewals and potential brief connectivity interruptions.

## Part 3: Routing Issues

15. A routing _______ occurs when packets endlessly circulate between routers without reaching their destination.
16. The TTL (Time to _______) field prevents routing loops by decrementing at each hop until reaching zero.
17. A _______ route entry points to a network that is unreachable, causing packets to be silently dropped.
18. An incorrect or missing _______ route prevents devices from reaching networks not in the routing table.
19. Route _______ occurs when multiple sources advertise conflicting paths with different metrics.
20. _______ routing entries that conflict with dynamic routing protocols can cause unexpected path selection.
21. Asymmetric routing occurs when traffic takes _______ paths for outbound and return traffic.

## Part 4: DNS Issues

22. DNS _______ failure prevents clients from translating hostnames to IP addresses.
23. A stale DNS _______ contains outdated IP address mappings that point to incorrect or decommissioned servers.
24. DNS _______ poisoning injects false records into a DNS resolver's cache, redirecting users to malicious sites.
25. An incorrect DNS _______ setting on a client prevents it from querying any DNS server.
26. A missing _______ DNS record means that a hostname cannot be resolved to its corresponding IP address.
27. Reverse DNS uses _______ (pointer) records to map IP addresses back to hostnames.
28. DNS _______ occurs when a query is forwarded to external DNS servers because the local server lacks the record.

## Part 5: VLAN and Switching Issues

29. A VLAN _______ prevents inter-VLAN communication since each VLAN is a separate broadcast domain.
30. A duplex _______ occurs when one side of a link uses full duplex and the other uses half duplex.
31. Duplex mismatches cause excessive _______ (detected on the full-duplex side) and collisions (on the half-duplex side).
32. An incorrect VLAN _______ on a switch port places a device in the wrong broadcast domain.
33. A missing or misconfigured _______ link between switches prevents VLAN traffic from crossing switch boundaries.
34. Native VLAN _______ between connected trunk ports can cause traffic to appear on the wrong VLAN.
35. Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) _______ can block ports unnecessarily, preventing legitimate traffic flow.
36. A _______ storm occurs when broadcast traffic loops endlessly through a switched network without STP.
37. Port _______ features can disable a switch port when policy violations are detected, blocking the connected device.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: IP Address Issues

1. An IP address **conflict** occurs when two devices on the same network are assigned the same IP address.
2. IP conflicts cause intermittent **connectivity** for one or both affected devices as they compete for the address.
3. A device with a **link-local** IP address (169.254.x.x) indicates it failed to obtain an address from DHCP.
4. APIPA stands for Automatic Private IP Address and uses the **169.254.0.0/16** address range.
5. An incorrect **subnet** mask causes devices to miscalculate which hosts are on the local network vs remote.
6. Assigning an IP address from the wrong **subnet** prevents the device from communicating on its intended network.
7. Duplicate **gateway** addresses on routers can cause packets to be forwarded to the wrong network.

## Part 2: DHCP Problems

8. DHCP **scope** exhaustion occurs when all available IP addresses in the scope have been assigned.
9. A **rogue** DHCP server is an unauthorized server that hands out incorrect network configuration.
10. DHCP **snooping** snooping is a switch security feature that filters untrusted DHCP messages.
11. A DHCP **relay** agent forwards DHCP requests between clients and servers across different subnets.
12. If a client receives DHCP settings with an incorrect default **gateway**, it cannot reach remote networks.
13. DHCP **lease** time determines how long a client keeps its assigned IP address before renewal.
14. Short DHCP lease times can cause frequent **address** renewals and potential brief connectivity interruptions.

## Part 3: Routing Issues

15. A routing **loop** occurs when packets endlessly circulate between routers without reaching their destination.
16. The TTL (Time to **Live**) field prevents routing loops by decrementing at each hop until reaching zero.
17. A **black hole** route entry points to a network that is unreachable, causing packets to be silently dropped.
18. An incorrect or missing **default** route prevents devices from reaching networks not in the routing table.
19. Route **redistribution** occurs when multiple sources advertise conflicting paths with different metrics.
20. **Static** routing entries that conflict with dynamic routing protocols can cause unexpected path selection.
21. Asymmetric routing occurs when traffic takes **different** paths for outbound and return traffic.

## Part 4: DNS Issues

22. DNS **resolution** failure prevents clients from translating hostnames to IP addresses.
23. A stale DNS **cache** contains outdated IP address mappings that point to incorrect or decommissioned servers.
24. DNS **cache** poisoning injects false records into a DNS resolver's cache, redirecting users to malicious sites.
25. An incorrect DNS **server** setting on a client prevents it from querying any DNS server.
26. A missing **A** DNS record means that a hostname cannot be resolved to its corresponding IP address.
27. Reverse DNS uses **PTR** (pointer) records to map IP addresses back to hostnames.
28. DNS **recursion** occurs when a query is forwarded to external DNS servers because the local server lacks the record.

## Part 5: VLAN and Switching Issues

29. A VLAN **boundary** prevents inter-VLAN communication since each VLAN is a separate broadcast domain.
30. A duplex **mismatch** occurs when one side of a link uses full duplex and the other uses half duplex.
31. Duplex mismatches cause excessive **late collisions** (detected on the full-duplex side) and collisions (on the half-duplex side).
32. An incorrect VLAN **assignment** on a switch port places a device in the wrong broadcast domain.
33. A missing or misconfigured **trunk** link between switches prevents VLAN traffic from crossing switch boundaries.
34. Native VLAN **mismatch** between connected trunk ports can cause traffic to appear on the wrong VLAN.
35. Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) **misconfiguration** can block ports unnecessarily, preventing legitimate traffic flow.
36. A **broadcast** storm occurs when broadcast traffic loops endlessly through a switched network without STP.
37. Port **security** features can disable a switch port when policy violations are detected, blocking the connected device.
hints: IP address conflicts occur when two devices share the same IP; APIPA (169.254.x.x) indicates DHCP failure.
An incorrect subnet mask causes devices to miscalculate local vs remote network boundaries.
Rogue DHCP servers hand out incorrect configurations; DHCP snooping filters untrusted DHCP messages on switches.
DHCP relay agents forward requests across subnets; scope exhaustion means all available IPs have been assigned.
Routing loops cause packets to circulate endlessly; TTL (Time to Live) prevents infinite loops by expiring packets.
A black hole route silently drops packets; a missing default route prevents reaching unknown networks.
DNS resolution failure prevents hostname-to-IP translation; PTR records provide reverse DNS (IP-to-hostname) lookup.
DNS cache poisoning injects false records; stale cache entries contain outdated IP mappings.
Duplex mismatches cause late collisions and errors; trunk link misconfigurations prevent cross-switch VLAN traffic.
Broadcast storms occur without STP; native VLAN mismatches between trunk ports cause traffic to leak between VLANs.
